title: Clarify what the connection lifecycle should be in the bindings

comments.body:
I don't know why we added the connection as part of the context. That seems like a bad decision to me, it would likely be much better if you just returned some struct{} from NewConnection() which holds the conn and then all bindings function just accepts that struct instead (i.e. we could just define them as methods but it wouldn't work across packages so it cannot work with the code structure)

Then we do not need GetClient() with the type check on the context each time. However I don't see this as an easy fix.

Adding a function to merge context would seem like an easy fix in the meantime.  But not sure how to best deal with it (I just found https://github.com/golang/go/issues/36503 from a quick search.)
I need to think more about it.

---

Overall I am not sure how worth it that connection sharing AFAIK is mostly a thing for the tcp connections. And we consider it insecure anyway as we prefer a unix socket and I don't think connections are shared over a unix socket.
However one obvious benifit to share the connection regardless is that we do not call /_ping every time to figure out the server version.

I thought a bit more about this issue.

First  I agree that while it'd probably be better if all bindings function just accepted a client struct rather than a context,  it's probably not worth to break backward compatibility for that. 

Now to make it easier to work with the context-based API, I'm not sure that merging context is really the right approach. 
The semantics of a merge operation are not that straightforward and can easily confuse a user (see the discussion in https://github.com/golang/go/issues/36503) 

What a user really wants to do is to take the request-scoped context that he has, and to transform it so it can be used in the bindings API. 

For that he only needs one thing: 
A helper `bindings.ContextWithClient(ctx context.Context, c bindings.Connection) context.Context` that simply injects the client in the context parameter via `context.WithValue()`

This change should be extremely minimal while enabling the user to properly use long-lived connections.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
